channel.title: Comments for page "The Stars Do Not Wait For You"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The Stars Do Not Wait For You"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tale is one of the best ones, I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the SCPs and their universe gets killed by the Nothing from Never-ending Story.

Just kidding, but the idea came to mind.

Y’know after the comment above reminded me of pattern screamers I got the idea of a power the ‘absence’ could be.

I thought, it must be the ‘Pattern’ itself. Either way +1 for me.
the desolation this non-thing leaves behind is beautifully written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly don't know what's more chilling. The idea of nothingness and everything ending in a moment, or the idea of the eternity that 1915 will probably have to experience after he reduced everything to nothing.

Truly thought evoking.

+1

item.title: A group of kactus' SCPs walk into a desert(Along with others)...
item.content:encoded:
Seeing as how this tale is about the end of the world brought on by nothing, what would the other nothings be like?

SCP-3000: If it really is Anantashesha, then it will exist in nothingness. Would it be possible an interaction between the two caused this end of the world?

SCP-3930: Literally nothing. Creates Pattern Screamers hateful of their own existence, and won't cease existing until those who created them go with them to the nothingness.

SCP-3812: Something constantly ascending. If the absence goes at everything, then what will stop it from catching something above narratives that mean nothing to it?

OUROBOROS: If the ends justify the means, what end justifies nothing? God-like Children, a tainted god, a narrative collapser, and The idea/nature of the Foundation itself, and this effectively causes their means to become unjustifiable. How can one justify nothing?

SCP-2747: How many star are waiting for him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this; really captured the spirit of the un-ness and not-ness as much as is allowed by using words.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed the writing, really moved the soul and all that good shit. Taking a benign SCP like 1915 and turning it into something like this is one of my favorite tropes (for lack of a better word). I also like the concept of a godly reality warper limiting their own abilities to something more understandable, something that they can control even if unconciously after the limits are set in place; good source of inspiration.

On another note, I expect an influx of new readers due to the Exploring Series new video on it, beyond what The Volgun already sent over lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept of nothing is scary enough, but to have nothing become something that comes towards you, ceaselessly and uncaringly regardless of whatever it is you throw at it, is truly terrifying. I feel as though this represents death, you can throw your entire life and your entire world away trying to stop it but it doesn't care, it comes for you and everyone and everything else all the same, a horrifying reality everyone has to grapple with eventually. Also, the line about it not mattering anyways with nothing surrounding them and coming towards them it might as well be here already, it feels like that extends to all of humanity throughout history, if it just ends like this, with nothing, humanity might as well have not existed at all, regardless of the countless struggles, triumphs, and tragedies that happen along the way, if it all amounts to nothing anyways was there a point to any of it? Similar vibes to the documents recovered from the marina trench in that way I suppose and something to really think about, does the end result even matter or is there value in the journey itself, even if it's bereft of meaning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of my interaction with SCP's has been through the Volgun's channel, including this story. Ive listened to his reading of this numerous times and always been curious about which SCPs were being discussed so thank you for the list at the start of this thread. In my attempts to introduce friends and family to the SCP universe this story has produced the strongest reactions out of any of the various tales and creatures I've used as a first impression. Even without having any previous introduction to or knowledge of the core foundation on which this is built it still seems to hit a nerve. The emotional impact far surpasses any confusion generated from a lack of familiarity with the source material. That is never easy to do. It is even harder to accomplish when the story is so far removed from reality and relatable experiences. Great story. Thank you for sharing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the easiest +1s I've ever given, and still one of my all-time favourite works on the entire site. There's something instantly gripping about the flow of the writing, and the way that so much of the actual spectacle is left purely up to the reader's imagination is brilliant. Whenever I think of End Of The World works set in the 'verse, this story is one of the first to come to mind, and it's more than earned that distinction.

Top-notch stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the plus side, at least every anomaly that the foundation needed to terminate but didn't have the means to is terminated now… on the flip side everyone is dead. Great read though.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. My. God. I have nothing to say except this may be the best tale I've ever read, end of story. I think you know what my vote is.

item.title: The All-mighty?
item.content:encoded: Who is the all-mighty? He is mentioned once in the entire site on this page. Who is it?

item.title: So good, i can't hold on.
item.content:encoded:
First, +1 for my respect. although i have just had a simple view about the CN-trsanlation of this page.

For me, this page is good because the romantic imagination: Fight between the reality-beanders, World's-end, and Lonelysess of The-Only-One. Waiting for the far away stars, with no one but the "bro"——it might also doesn't exist.

Broken sky fly to the other space in a another shape, don't wait for stars, they are waiting you.

Maybe i got a wrong point but i really like the thing i got now, soooo cooooool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those pieces that, no matter how many times I read it, always causes a huge emotional reaction. That strong and visceral reaction is why I enjoy this tale immensely. And a lot of it is tied to how the whole thing, particularly the Absence, is presented. Why does to Absence destroy everything? Because it does. Why is the Absence so powerful? Because it is. Why can't anybody defeat it? Because they can't. The fact that a thing like this, with no buildup, reason, or motivation, overcomes all the people and groups with deep motivations and histories, provokes emotions of utter frustration and sadness. The lack of a deeper motivation and the pointless atmosphere of the whole thing is what really hammers in the emotion of the whole work.

Not to mention the fantastic writing present throughout the whole thing and the vivid descriptions of the various groups/individuals/beings involved. I particularly found the pairing of Lombardi and Nobody utterly brilliant (if you don't get it, read Nobody Knows).

item.title: Simply Impressed
item.content:encoded:
I really can only say wow.
TheVolgun's reading of this was the first ever Tale I'd ever seen, and it absolutely hooked me into the wiki.

Working on an idea which will involve this tale; hopefully, I can do it justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can never place into words how this tale makes me feel. There's only one word I can use to describe the story itself:

…haunting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least 682 is dead, since it's not mentioned anywhere in this tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After seeing so many ways the SCP universe could meet it's end, I never thought it could be so sad to see it go one more time. Depressing stuff usually isn't my forte, but this tale is just so damn well written, and feels like an appropriately grim ending to a universe filled with world ending monsters. I especially love how it manages to not depower the scips to justify their lose against the Absence, which would have felt kinda cheap. Also, it nice the tale didn't just conveniently forget 343's existence to justify it's events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that huge smily face shaped area that wanders around and converts all the air to poison thus killing everything in it's path

: )

Nah, the Absence is not really very much like that at all. In any case, as kaktus says, this is not a story about how the Foundation comes up with clever ideas to keep the flame alive. This is a story about when losing is inevitable. The beauty to be found in the fire going out is a theme that both Dmatix and Djoric return to regularly, and this is a quality example of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would argue that the nature of the Absence is unlike literally any of the articles you mentioned, and that the nature of the Absence really isn't even the point of this tale. If you want to take the absolute simplest approach to what is generally regarded as a masterwork from one of the site's most lauded authors, then you're certainly free to do so. But please understand that your decision to view this from the most basic point of view does not adequately reflect the craftsmanship on display here, nor should it give anyone any room to dismiss the subjects of the tale as "a buncha idiots".

In other words, with such a limited point of view it's extremely difficult to take this kind of critique seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I couldn't take this one seriously. The foundation has encountered anomalies like this before, like the shadow child, 2470, some infohazards, 3930, that huge smily face shaped area that wanders around and converts all the air to poison thus killing everything in it's path. Honestly this sort of thing is part of why they focus on the larger concept of containment. They could have moved out of the way, they could have left, but no, they found what is basically a hole in the ground and they just tryed the same thing over and over and doing it bigger when it didn't work last time, tost all their stuff in, convinced their friends to jump in, got some more stuff to throw at it then jumped in themselves. Like a buncha idiots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was fantastic, my favorite end of earth tale on the site probably.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So wait, is this thing like a Neverwere or…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An interesting way to think of this (I think, and I know it's not the original intention) is as a progression of 1915's powers if you see what I mean. After this, he could probably live a new mediocre, unchanging life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tale is fantastic, and underrated. The whole article is powerful, but the part where the city is obliterated really got me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God-tier tale. Everything is done so well in this crushing and heart-wrenching way, yet every character is one you can feel for, no matter how small their presence is in the tale. +77 is not enough for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The story is masterfully written, like all your stories. But…there are already so many "the little SCP that Really Could End The World" stories. Maybe I'm just not a fan of the nihilistic stuff, but my reaction was "Whelp, everyone sure did done died." Although the descriptions of everyone fighting the Absence were awesome. No vote, because it's unfair to downvote something this well-written and well-conceived just because I don't like the effect you were going for here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Hateful Star paused in its advance as the world was consumed by an Absence. It hadn't expected this. It, a being with the power to pull apart the strands of time itself and peer into the future, had been misled.

"Awwwww, balls.", it pulsed to itself. And then;

"Come on! Really? Fourty years of work down the drain? After all that?! Son of a-"

Consumed with rage, it fled to a stellar coffee bar near Regulus where it spent several aeons imbibing heavily and writing Why Wasn't Your Puny Little World Next You Bastards, a volume of poetry which was widely well-received, if often criticized for its profane tone. The Star, having put its angry youth behind it, took a correspondence course in auto mechanics and settled down. Today, SCP-1548 lives happily with its Class-O main-sequence wife, two binary children, and a pet comet in a condominium on the quieter side of Sagittarius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit, this is sublime. The sheer amount of meticulous detail you put into this, the vivid imagery, and the raw emotions conveyed through this that you capture perfectly made me tear up; I admit it. The desperation of the Foundation; the tragedy of Cain and Able; the reluctant acceptance of the survivors huddled in the fortress (that part nearly had me bawling, by the way); everything was conveyed beautifully. Congratulations, Dmatix; another Tale has been inducted into my Hall of Fame.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation was desperate enough that they tried everything they had in order to stop the Absence, including previously forbidden practices like those you mentioned, yes. It did them little good by the end. As for the Hateful Star, it might still be coming, but as the ending suggests, the Absence is now coming at everything else. If/when it will meet it, the Star's end will be the same as the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't matter. All that's left is dust. Even a no-longer-Broken God could not stop this. No God could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, was the Broken God remade when the Foundation teamed up with the Cotbg, or after the Foundation released all the SCPs?

Also, since its the end of the world, and the more power you send the better, could there be some insane cross-testing? like, 036 and 106, or encouraging the repairs of 2399?

Finally, would the Crab Nebula keep coming after everyone is dead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote. Just…upvote.

…Lord have mercy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I could vicariously feel the Foundation's desperation as this story progressed. This is probably the most emotionally crushing Tale I've ever read here. There's been apocalypse before, there's been SCP's powerless before the storm before, but this is on a whole other level. If I could +1 this indefinitely I would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, so it was. I'm terribly sorry; I always confuse you two. I think it's the 'D' that does it. My sincerest apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote if you cry every time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not usually one for emotions. A infant cries, a puppy whines, a reject sighs, yet I don't react. I don't care for them.

This took my ever present scowl off of my face. This is only one of a few things I have ever read that were able to do that. This is masterfully written. I don't have much else to say, it works brilliantly on every single level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pssssst Et Tam was me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I think struck me the most about this was the fall of Cain and Able. Those two are a walking tragedy all on their, but we're very rarely presented with a situation that makes that apparent, much adds to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, it's neat and pleasingly pointless in its almightiness, but I wouldn't say it was the most amazing thing on the site, nor even my favourite Dmatix piece (which has got to be the climax of Et Tam [actually written by Djoric. Whoops]). Not to detract from this, I do rather like it. Just for a bit of balance :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By all accounts, I should not have been able to see you, for my apartment was truly a tiny containment cell about half a mile underground, but such considerations never meant much to one living entirely in his own world.

That sums up how powerful and unsettling time and reality-bending SCPs can be right there, in one sentence. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now I see why everyone was all over this last night in chat. One of the best tales I've read on this site. Using 1915 was mad genius, and I can't wait to go through that list upthread. Fantastic.

Edit: I feel I should mention this is the first time any mention of Pangloss has done anything for me. The way he's described… well, I wish that had come about sooner, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a masterpiece. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahh well that certainly makes sense.

Now I feel like someone who complains because Schindler's List didn't have enough esplosions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A good story could certainly be written about an event like this being the result of 231-7's offspring being born, but it wouldn't be the story I wanted to tell here. The reason I chose 1915 for this is exactly because it's unassuming and benign; it's about the world ending for no particular reason, after all, because of something without a goal and without motivation. Had it been something related to 231, for example, there would be no avoiding a certain pathos to the entire thing- after all, 231 has plenty of possible reasons to destroy the world, for vengeance due to the way they were treated to the simple fact that they were created to bring about the end of the world. 1915 has nothing to do with the end of the world, which is why, in this story, it was the one to bring it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit, when I started reading I did not expect what I got. I felt it was a good story, end of the world and all that…but I kept reading. You've been able to convey emotion and feeling superbly. Desperation. Sadness. Resignation.

Bravo. Great job, mate. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this, all except for the fact that it all inexplicably stemmed from a rather random and kinda boring scip. If I had read this backwards I would probably have guessed it was 231-7's baby or something a little more … awesome.

Apart from that I had a little trouble keeping up with the passage of time, at some points it seems only a couple of months have passed but then at the end it seems to be years and years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, this didn't quite do it for me. Of your three apocalypse tales, I found this the weakest. I just am not sure what the idea is. Some unstoppable force of annihilation is unleashed out of a drab reality bender, and it gradually consumes the world? It's well-written, but more admirable than interesting or enjoyable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I…truly don't know what to say about this. This is…amazing. Phenomenal. Powerful. Terrifying. It is probably the best work I have ever seen on the site, and if it isn't, it is very close. You literally took the concept of Nothing…and made it more terrifying than it already is. Dmatix, I don't know that I've ever told you how good of a writer you are, but this is freaking amazing.

+1. I've never wanted so badly to upvote a tale a second time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Powerful *wipes away a tear* …powerful

+1'd. You deserve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right. That's it. You win. What do you win? You just… you just win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A perfected punch to the soul.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you had asked me what could defeat or destroy every last person, group, and anomaly on the SCPverse's Earth, I would have told you "nothing".

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure thing:

By order of appearance:

- SCP-1915
- SCP-1935
- SCP-1440
- SCP-963
- Pangloss (various)
- SCP-076
- SCP-073
- SCP-343
- SCP-2845

And various human characters appearing in tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I said it in chat, but it bears repeating here.
This piece is incredible on every level. You should be very proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderful. Would you mind giving us a list of all the SCPs referenced in this? I got a bunch of the obvious ones, but…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This tale (and holy fuck, that title) hit me pretty damn hard.

The best among us trypoor panny, he tried so hard and fail.

We gotta work faster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tale took quite a while to write. Not because of its length, which is nothing out of the ordinary, but because of the subject matter. I've written about the apocalypse a few times before, but never quite in this manner, I think.
